10 min read
Just now

--

10-minute read on how Compute Express Link is quietly rewiring the modern data center

For the better part of four decades, computers have been built around a simple, unquestioned assumption: memory lives right next to the CPU that uses it.
DRAM sticks plug into slots a few inches from the processor socket, wired up so tightly that the physical distance between them is measured in centimeters, not meters.
It’s a design that made sense when workloads were predictable and servers were general-purpose boxes doing a bit of everything.
That assumption is now breaking down — and an interconnect standard called Compute Express Link (CXL) is the industry’s answer to fixing it.
The Problem: Memory That’s There but Unreachable
Picture a typical cloud server: two 64-core CPUs, 512GB of RAM. It sounds generous, but real workloads rarely use resources evenly.
A video transcoding job might peg every CPU core while barely touching memory. An in-memory database might demand a full terabyte of RAM while the CPU sits mostly idle.
Because a server’s compute and memory are welded together at manufacturing time, neither job can borrow what the other doesn’t need.
The result is a phenomenon researchers call “stranded memory” — DRAM that’s installed, powered on, and fully paid for, but functionally inaccessible because the CPU cores next to it are maxed out on something else.
A study of Microsoft Azure’s production clusters, analyzing traces from 100 clusters over 75 days, found that as much as 25% of total DRAM capacity sits stranded in highly utilized deployments. That’s a serious problem when DRAM makes up roughly half the total cost of building a server in the first place.
“In highly utilized cloud clusters, up to 25% of total DRAM is stranded — powered on, paid for, and inaccessible.”
Underneath this economic issue lies a physical one. The parallel DDR interface that connects CPUs to memory is expensive in terms of processor pins — about 200 pins to deliver roughly 50GB/s of bandwidth.
Serial interconnects like PCIe achieve nearly five times that bandwidth density using a fraction of the pins. DDR also can’t tolerate distance: signal integrity requirements force memory to stay within inches of the CPU, capping how much a single server can ever hold.
And when engineers have tried to solve capacity problems over the network instead — using TCP/IP and RDMA — the software stack adds 10+ microseconds of latency, hopelessly slow for the sub-4KB, millisecond-critical requests that modern AI and search workloads generate constantly.
Large language models make all of this worse. Inference is dominated by the token-by-token “generation phase,” an operation that is memory-bandwidth-bound rather than compute-bound — the GPU spends most of its time waiting for weights to arrive from memory, not computing on them.
High-bandwidth memory (HBM) is fast, but a single GPU might only carry 80GB of it, nowhere near enough for trillion-parameter models. This mismatch between HBM’s capacity and the model’s appetite is what’s now called the “bandwidth wall.”
Key takeaway: Fixed CPU-to-memory ratios strand huge amounts of expensive DRAM, and neither faster DDR nor traditional networking can fix it — the industry needed a fundamentally different interconnect.
Enter CXL: Rethinking What Memory Can Be
CXL is an open industry-standard interconnect that rides on the same physical cabling and connectors as PCIe 5.0/6.0, but adds a smarter protocol layer on top.
Rather than treating memory as something bolted permanently to one CPU, CXL treats it as a shareable resource that can be pooled, sliced, and reassigned across an entire rack.
Under the hood, CXL multiplexes three sub-protocols over a single physical link:

• CXL.io handles the basics — device discovery, configuration, and interrupt management. It’s functionally identical to standard PCIe and ensures backward compatibility.

• CXL.cache lets external accelerators — SmartNICs, custom silicon — snoop directly into the CPU’s cache hierarchy using the same MESI coherency states (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) that CPU cores use among themselves.

• CXL.mem is the star of the show: it lets the host CPU issue ordinary load/store instructions to memory sitting on a completely separate device, as if it were local RAM.
Devices are categorized by which protocols they use.
Type 1 devices (like SmartNICs) use .io and .cache to process data without owning memory of their own. Type 2 devices (GPUs, FPGAs) use all three protocols, sharing their own onboard memory with the host. Type 3 devices — pure memory expanders — use .io and .mem only, and they're the ones responsible for the pooling and rack-scale architectures everyone's excited about.
Physically, data moves in fixed 68-byte “flits”: a 2-byte header identifying the payload type, a 64-byte data payload sized to exactly match a CPU cache line, and 2 bytes of error-checking.
Critically, CXL.mem and CXL.cache traffic gets routed through a specialized low-latency path that bypasses the heavier PCIe transaction logic — which is how CXL manages round-trip latencies of roughly 170–250 nanoseconds, in the same ballpark as reaching across a NUMA socket to a second CPU, and far faster than PCIe’s 500+ nanosecond DMA transfers.
“CXL doesn’t invent a new physical connector — it reuses PCIe’s wires and adds a protocol smart enough to make remote memory feel local.”
Key takeaway: CXL borrows PCIe’s physical layer but adds a coherency-aware protocol stack, letting a CPU treat memory on a separate device almost as if it were sitting in a local DIMM slot.
What CXL Actually Unlocks
The technical elegance matters less than what it enables in practice.
Hardware cache coherency means external devices can safely cache system memory without constant manual flushing. “Bias modes” let the system decide, per memory region, whether the CPU or the device controls coherency — device-heavy AI training workloads benefit enormously from letting the accelerator take ownership and rip through reads and writes without checking in with the CPU each time.
Media abstraction decouples the CPU’s memory controller from the specific physics of any one memory type. Instead of hardcoding DDR timings into the processor, CXL lets a controller on the device itself translate generic load/store commands into whatever the underlying memory requires — DDR4, DDR5, low-power DDR, or emerging non-volatile memory — all without redesigning the CPU.
Memory pooling and disaggregation are the headline act. CXL 2.0 introduced Multi-Logical Devices: a single 1TB memory card can be sliced into up to 16 independent partitions and dynamically handed out to different servers by a piece of orchestration software called the Fabric Manager.
A database server can grab 200GB of extra capacity during a traffic spike and release it back to the shared pool overnight — no over-provisioning required, no wasted DRAM sitting stranded.
And because CXL relies on ordinary CPU load/store instructions instead of network packets, it achieves something RDMA never could: zero-copy, kernel-bypassing access to remote memory, with latency roughly 40x lower than a typical RDMA round trip.
One-sentence takeaway: CXL turns memory from a fixed, per-server asset into a dynamic, rack-level resource that can be coherently shared, sliced, and reassigned on demand.
Three Generations, Three Leaps
CXL’s short history maps neatly onto three specification releases.
CXL 1.0/1.1 (2019) solved a narrow problem: attaching coherent memory directly to a single CPU’s root port. No switching, no pooling — just point-to-point expansion within one server.
CXL 2.0 (2020) is where pooling was born. It introduced the CXL switch, enabling multiple hosts to share a pool of memory devices through a single-level “star” topology, along with the Fabric Manager software that binds and unbinds memory slices on demand.
CXL 3.0 (2022) turned CXL into a true rack-scale fabric capable of connecting up to 4,096 nodes.
It doubled the data rate to 64 GT/s using PAM4 signaling, introduced multi-level switching for building spine-leaf network topologies, and — perhaps most importantly — enabled genuine memory sharing rather than just pooling.
Earlier generations let one host own a memory region at a time; CXL 3.0’s “Back-Invalidate” protocol lets multiple hosts read and write the same address, with the memory device itself acting as referee, snooping other hosts to force a cache flush before a conflicting write proceeds.
CXL 3.0 also enables peer-to-peer communication, letting a GPU pull data straight from a memory pool through the switch without routing through the host CPU at all.
“CXL 1.0 solved one server’s memory problem. CXL 3.0 is trying to solve the whole rack’s.”
One-sentence takeaway: Each CXL generation widened the blast radius of what memory could be shared — from one server, to a small pool, to a multi-thousand-node fabric with true simultaneous sharing.
Does It Actually Work? What the Research Shows
Academic and industry studies over the past few years have started putting real numbers behind the promise.
The Microsoft Azure/Intel study mentioned earlier didn’t just quantify the stranding problem — it also modeled the economics of fixing it.
It concluded that a hybrid deployment (60–80% local DRAM, 20–40% pooled CXL memory) offers the best trade-off, since 21–37% of workloads see meaningful performance degradation when memory latency doubles, which is roughly the CXL penalty.
A project called NeoMem tackled a subtler issue: how do you know which pages of memory are “hot” and deserve to stay in fast local DRAM versus “cold” pages that can be demoted to CXL?
Traditional software profiling burns CPU cycles scanning page tables. NeoMem instead offloaded that tracking to hardware inside the CXL controller itself, using a Count-Min Sketch algorithm to monitor every single memory access at essentially zero CPU cost (0.021% overhead versus 5–10% for software approaches) — and delivered 32–67% speedups over existing tiering methods as a result.
MemTunnel flipped the pooling model on its head, letting servers within a rack lend each other local DRAM directly, host-to-host, rather than relying on a dedicated memory appliance — achieving roughly 44% speedup over RDMA-based pooling.
A separate effort explored tunneling CXL traffic over standard 100GbE Ethernet to break past CXL’s native ~3-meter distance limit, achieving a 1.97-microsecond round trip that, while slower than native CXL, still beat RDMA by 37%.
Perhaps most striking is Samsung’s CXL-PNM (“processing-near-memory”) work aimed squarely at LLM inference.
By embedding compute logic directly into a CXL memory controller loaded with 512GB of LPDDR5X, the system offloads the memory-bound matrix-vector multiplication step of token generation onto the memory device itself — the CPU sends an input vector and receives back a finished result.
The payoff: a 4.4x increase in token generation throughput and a 53% cut in energy consumption.
“The CPU sends the input vector, and the memory device computes the result locally — returning only the final token.”
Key takeaway: Independent research across cloud economics, hardware tiering, host-to-host pooling, and LLM inference all points the same direction — CXL delivers real, measurable gains, not just theoretical ones.
The Catch: CXL Isn’t Free Speed
None of this comes without trade-offs, and it’s worth being honest about them.
Every hop away from local DRAM costs latency. Local DDR5 runs at roughly 70–80 nanoseconds; a direct-attach CXL device adds around 57ns for a total of ~170ns; route through a switch and you’re looking at 250–300ns for rack-scale deployments.
That’s a 2–3x latency penalty compared to memory sitting right next to the CPU, and it means CXL can’t simply replace local DIMMs everywhere.
It’s a complement, not a wholesale substitute, best suited to a tiered architecture where hot data stays local and col
